title: Distribution Beats Talent: The New Rules of Value in an AI Economy

source.original: You Have 10 Years to Join the AI Economy | Michael Saylor

body_html:
In a world where AI can replicate Shakespearean sonnets, draft complex legal documents, and generate medical opinions at near-zero marginal cost, the traditional formula for economic success — develop a talent, work hard, get paid — is being systematically dismantled. The new winners won't be the most skilled individuals. They'll be the ones who control distribution, influence, and networks.

The logic is straightforward. If you have a social media account with five million followers, you can take an AI-generated video and monetize it instantly across a massive audience. If you're the person who painstakingly created that video by hand but have fifty followers, you can't monetize anything. Apple has a billion devices in pockets worldwide — they can distribute any AI application to a billion people overnight. Amazon has last-mile logistics to hundreds of millions of households and the backend infrastructure to capitalize on robots and self-driving vehicles when they arrive. The value accrues to the distribution layer, not the production layer.

This represents a fundamental inversion of how most people think about career building. The conventional path — spend years accumulating credentials, develop deep expertise, trade hours for income — was rational when human skill was the scarce input. But when AI delivers ten thousand lawyers for ten dollars a month, the scarcity shifts from skill to reach. The question is no longer what can you do but how many people can you reach with what the AI does.

The practical advice is uncomfortable but clear: invest in assets rather than credentials. If given the choice between spending £200,000 on a professional degree or acquiring £200,000 worth of scarce desirable property, the property may be the better bet. The value of the degree will be eroded by automation; the property compounds as currency debases. Similarly, building an audience, a brand, or a distribution channel may matter more than mastering any particular technical skill.

The ten-year window matters because robotics will extend this dynamic from white-collar to blue-collar work. Today, AI is automating knowledge work. Within a decade, physical robots at perhaps $100 per month in rental cost will handle construction, moving, painting, and transportation. Anyone whose value proposition is I work hard or I'm talented without an accompanying distribution advantage will find themselves competing against machines that never sleep, never complain, and cost almost nothing to operate.
